The fortress of Qasdar can be seen to the right of the image, with several archers on its battlements, with the troops of Sebuktegin, also armed, to the left of the scene. Folio 151v Rustam and Subrab in Battle The First Day

value.en: Folio 151v Rustam and Subrab in Battle The First Day

As the Iranian army masses before the White Castle, Suhrab charges the shah's tent. Only Rustam, not knowing Suhrab's identity, accepts his challenge. The com bat that ensues lasts all day but ends in a draw. With the armies thus engaged, Rustam rides oll in pursuit of the Turanians, and Suhrab gallops in the direction of the Iranians.

en: Folio 67v Zal Receives Mibrabs Homage at Kabul

value.en: Folio 67v Zal Receives Mibrabs Homage at Kabul

On a tour of his territories, Zal visits Kabul, where he entertains Mihrab, the local king and a descendant of Zahhak. Mihrab presents many gifts, but Zal is most intrigued by the description of Mihrab's beautiful daughter.

en: Folio 164v_note Sudabas Second Accusation Against Siyavush is Judged

value.en: Folio 164v_note Sudabas Second Accusation Against Siyavush is Judged

Sudaba gets hold of stillborn twins, claiming they were me result of her rape by Siyavush and that their miscarriage was brought about by her distress. Kai Kavus now summons his astrologers, but they rule that the twins could not be children of Kai Kavus or Sudaba. She accuses the astrologers of lying in fear of punishment from Siyavush and Rustam.

en: Folio 174r Kai Kavus Upbraids Siyavush in a Letter

value.en: Folio 174r Kai Kavus Upbraids Siyavush in a Letter

When Rustam brings news of the peace to Kai Kavus, the shah refuses to believe the gesture is in good faith. He relieves Rustam of his command of the army and appoints Tus in his place. Kai Kavus drafts a furious letter to Siyavush, accusing his son of laziness and of scheming with Rustam to overthrow him. He urges Siyavush to recommence war with Turan. Rustam returns to Zabulistan washing his hands of Kai Kavus.

The hero Feridun enters the story towards the beginning, when the evil king Zahak dreams that Feridun would strike him down with an ox-headed mace and put an end to his reign of darkness. Feridun, whose father had been killed by Zahak, wages various campaigns against the tyrant and even manages to enthrone himself in Zahak's castle

en: Folio 438r Isfandiyars Sixth Course He Comes Through the Snow

value.en: Folio 438r Isfandiyars Sixth Course He Comes Through the Snow

After three days and nights of ceaseless snow and terrible cold, everyone in Isfandiyar’s army turned to prayer. Instantly the clouds blew away, and they gave thanks to God. Here, Isfandiyar’s followers prepare for the storm, warming themselves beside campfires and securing their tents. Most scenes in the Shahnama have no reference to weather. Either the sun shines or the stars twinkle in the sky. Here, however, the artist successfully conveys the sense of the beginning of a heavy snowfall against a gray sky.

en: Folio 44v Faridun Receives a Message from Salm and Tur

value.en: Folio 44v Faridun Receives a Message from Salm and Tur

Faridun divides his kingdom among his sons, Salm receives Rum, the Byzantine empire; Tur rules Central Asia and China, a land known as Turan; and to Iraj falls Iran. In their distant dominions Salm and Tur grow envious of Iraj and, plotting, send a messenger to threaten. Faridun with war unless Iraj is also sent abroad.

When the army of Salm and Tur has crossed the Oxus River, Faridun sends Manuchihr at the head of a powerful force to meet them. With the battle lines drawn, Qubad, son of Kava, leads the Iranian vanguard. Belore the battle can begin, Tur rides forth and questions Manuchihr's right to the Iranian throne.Qubad agrees to deliver the message.

Two shahs, Zav and Garshasp, succeed Nauzar, but neither reign lasts long, and no new shah is appointed after them. Zal continues as chief paladin but is blamed for the adversity Iran has suffered. Zal agrees and suggests that Rustam, his son, join him at the head of the army. First, Rustam has to find a suitable horse. In the herds of Zabul is a bay called Rakhsh that has never been caught. Rustam lassoes him and finds his mount tor life.

In the reign of Hushang, grandson of Gayumars, the world came to understand the usefulness of minerals and the arts of smithery, agriculture, and irrigation. One day, Hushang spied a dragon lurking behind the rocks. He hurled a stone at it, which missed the monster but hit a larger rock, causing sparks to fly. Realizing the significance of this phenomenon, Hushang built a large fire and held a feast to celebrate its discovery. The witty yet benevolent depictions of people and animals characterize the liveliest of Sultan_Muhammad ’s creations. Also typical of his style is the oval composition with mountains rising into the margins of the page.

AKM155 (folio 37 verso) depicts the terrible fate of Zahhak, who, as a young man, came under the spell of the evil Ahriman. Ahriman appeared to Zahhak in the form of a congenial fellow and convinced Zahhak to murder his father, an Arabian king. Once Zahhak had taken control of his father’s lands, Ahriman reappeared in a different guise, this time as a cook.

en: Folio 86v The Shahs Wise Men Approve of Zals Marriage

value.en: Folio 86v The Shahs Wise Men Approve of Zals Marriage

Zal was the son of Sam of the house of Nariman, the ruling family of Sistan who had been vassals and faithful supporters of the Iranian crown for a long time. During his travels to the east, Zal fell in love with the beautiful Rudaba, daughter of the ruler of the rival kingdom of Kabul. But when he expressed his desire to marry her, Shah Manuchihr became very concerned about the future of the Iranian kingdom and reacted negatively to his plans. Upon Sam's insistence, however, the shah asked the advice of his sages and astrologers. Luckily the stars and the wise men gave a positive verdict: not only would the marriage be a felicitous event, but the union of the Iranian prince and the Indian princess would bear the most important Iranian hero, Rustam. This is a lavish enthronement scene, a rather common iconographic subject in the Persian tradition. The artists used this occasion to construct an elaborate interior space, whose tiled surfaces and painted walls probably closely resemble contemporary royal interiors. They also included references to two other important elements of Iranian royal architecture: fountains and gardens.

en: Folio 339r Planning for the Joust of the Twelve Rooks

value.en: Folio 339r Planning for the Joust of the Twelve Rooks

In reaction to the stalemate between their armies, Gudarz and Piran hold a parlev and agree on a series of eleven jousts to decide the winner of the war. In cach camp the commanders choose the ten champions who, in addition to themselves, will oust. “As a venue, they select a field between two mounds so that the winners can plant their flags on their respective peaks

en: Folio 18v Firdausis Parable of the Ship of Shiism

value.en: Folio 18v Firdausis Parable of the Ship of Shiism

In the introduction to the Shahnama, God launched seventy ships, bearing adherents to the religions of mankind, into a stormy sea. Clearly depicted on the central ship are the Prophet and his son-in-law and successor Ali sitting beneath the red canopy, while Ali's sons, Hasan and Husain, stand nearby. Details such as the inlaid stars and figures with very small elliptical pupils are characteristic of works attributed to Mirza 'Ali, the son of the master painter Sultan_Muhammad .

At a ceremony where Zahhak forces his noblemen to sign a scroll proclaiming, his justice and honesty, the blacksmith Kava protests his last living son's being, chosen to be ted to Zahhak's snakes. He then rips the scroll to shreds.

en: Folio 63v Sam Comes to Mount Alburs

value.en: Folio 63v Sam Comes to Mount Alburs

Word of the child on Mount Alburz reaches Sam and, prompted by a dream he goes to the mountain to search for his son. Looking up at the simurgh's nest, he spies Zal but cannot climb up to reach him. The simurgh reluctantly brings Zal to his father, giving the boy a feather to burn when he needs the simurgh's help.

en: Folio 195r Siyavush Recounts His Nightmare to Farangis

value.en: Folio 195r Siyavush Recounts His Nightmare to Farangis

Siyavush, a Persian prince, awoke one night from a horrible nightmare. The dream, which proved to be prophetic, showed the destruction of his kingdom and foreshadowed the end of his life. Attributed to Qadimi and Mir Musavvir, the second supervisor of the Shahnama project, this folio combines the spatial logic of the Herat style with the elongated figures typical of Safavid painting under Shah Isma'il.

en: Folio 218r Kay Khusraw Welcomed by his Grandfather, Kay Kaus, King of Iran

value.en: Folio 218r Kay Khusraw Welcomed by his Grandfather, Kay Kaus, King of Iran

The scene is enacted at the court of Kay Kaus. He is seated on his throne together with his grandson, Kay Khusraw, who has just returned from Turan, where he grew up in secrecy after his father, good Prince Siyawush, was killed.

en: Folio 343r The Sixth Joust of the Rooks Bizhan Versus Ruyin

value.en: Folio 343r The Sixth Joust of the Rooks Bizhan Versus Ruyin

In the sixth joust Bizhan, Giv's son, takes on Ruyin, son of Piran. When arrows make no dent in the armor of either soldier, Bizhan steers his horse alongside Ruyin and crashes his mace down on his foe's head. Ruyin remains in his saddle at first but soon falls, his skull smashed to bits.

en: Folio 34v Faridun Enthroned in the Palace of Zahhak

value.en: Folio 34v Faridun Enthroned in the Palace of Zahhak

Having seized Zahhak's palace and throne, Faridun finds the daughters of Jamshid, to whom he introduces religion. Although Zahhak had fled before Faridun's arrival, his wily vizier Kundrau soon arrives, unaware of what has transpired

en: Folio 153v Suhrab Gains the Upper Hana The Second Day

value.en: Folio 153v Suhrab Gains the Upper Hana The Second Day

Rustam continues to conceal his identity from Suhrab and imagines his son to be sate with his mother. Thus, they again light all through the day until Suhrab lifts Rustam by the belt and throws him to the ground. When Suhrab draws his dagger, Rustam says that the rules of wrestling require him to be thrown down twice before the coup de grace can be dealt. Suhrab believes him, and they agree to resume the next day.

en: Folio 138r Pilsam Fights Four of the Seven Champions

value.en: Folio 138r Pilsam Fights Four of the Seven Champions

The forces of Afrasiyab vastly outnumber Rustam and the champions, but the Iranians bravely scatter hundreds of their foes. Then Afrasiyab dispatches Pilsam, a mighty warrior, who parries the blows of four champions. Undeterred, they regroup and rout the Turanians

en: Folio 345r The Tenth Joust of the Rooks, Barta versus Kuhram

value.en: Folio 345r The Tenth Joust of the Rooks, Barta versus Kuhram

With the eighth and ninth jousts the results are the same: more dead Turanians and more banners on the Iranian mound. In the tenth contest Barta of Iran and Kuhram of Turan confront one another, swords in hand. With a nimble shift of his horse, Barta cleaves Kuhram in two with his Indian scimitar.

en: Folio 472r Rustam Avenges His Own Impending Death

value.en: Folio 472r Rustam Avenges His Own Impending Death

Rustam's wicked half-brother Shaghad conceived a plan to kill him by preparing a hunting ground with pits filled with upright spears. When Rustam went hunting near these traps, he fell into a pit and was impaled. Realizing that Shaghad, watching from behind a tree, had devised the plot, Rustam sent an arrow through the tree and killed Shaghad, shortly before he himself died. Although the figures are devoid of emotion, the barren setting accentuates the starkness and finality of the scene.

en: Folio 535r Hurmuzd Is Last Testament to Prince Bahram I

value.en: Folio 535r Hurmuzd Is Last Testament to Prince Bahram I

Shapur and Mihrak's daughter have a son, who rules as Hurmuzd I, a just and prudent king. When he grows old and death is upon him, he calls for the crown prince Bahram I and exhorts him to rule with moderation.

en: Folio 146r Rustam Pained Before Kay Kavus

value.en: Folio 146r Rustam Pained Before Kay Kavus

Kai Kavus is sufficiently worried about the new Turanian threat that he sends the knight Giv to summon Rustam and prepare for war. Rustam dismisses the urgency of the request and delays his departure. Finally, when he and Giv appear before Kai Kavus, the shah harangues them both, and Rustam leaves in a fit of pique. Eventually the paladins intervene, and Rustam returns to offer his obeisance to Kai Kavus

en: Folio 236r The Iranians Mourn Farud and Jarira

value.en: Folio 236r The Iranians Mourn Farud and Jarira

The Shahnama is filled with tragic events and moments of intense drama. On the page illustrated here, the painters have depicted the mourning of Iranians over the tragic destiny of Farud, the son of Prince Siyavush, and Jarira, his mother. After witnessing her son's death, Jarira ripped open her belly with a dagger. Then, lying down with her face against Farud's, she let herself die. The scene is one of the most emotionally charged in the entire manuscript. The women pull out their hair and cry, the courtiers beat their heads in despair, while the the creased expressions on their faces convey intense compassion. Even though Islamic painting is considered to be idealized, the painters of this Shahnama reveal instead a profound awareness of human emotions, which are depicted with great sensitivity.

en: Folio 248r Giv Avenges Bahram by Slaying Tazhav

value.en: Folio 248r Giv Avenges Bahram by Slaying Tazhav

In this human form restored, Faridun greets his sons and admits his ruse. With new insight mto their characters, he names them: the eldest one, who sought safety, is called Salm; the next, who did not shrink from a fight, is Tur; and the third, most sensible son is named Iraj.

en: Folio 341v The First Joust of the Rooks Fariburz versus Kalbad

value.en: Folio 341v The First Joust of the Rooks Fariburz versus Kalbad

The first rivals are Fariburz, uncle of Kay Khusrau, and Kalbad, brother of Piram When the arrows Fariburz shoots at his foe miss their mark, he draws his sword and slices Kalbad in two, from neck to waist. He then ascends the mound and places the first Iranian flag on it

en: Folio 30v Zahhak slays the sacred cow Barmayah

value.en: Folio 30v Zahhak slays the sacred cow Barmayah

Soon after the birth of Faridun, Zahhak finds the boy's family and kills his father. The bov's mother escapes with her baby but delivers him to a cowherd, who feeds Faridun with the milk ofhis cow, Birmaya. After three vears Zahhak tracks down the cow and slays her, but Faridun has already Hed to safety.

en: Folio 475r Faramarz Encircled While Battling Bahman

value.en: Folio 475r Faramarz Encircled While Battling Bahman

Bahman, the son of Isfandiyar, wished to avenge his father’s death by destroying Zabulistan (Sistan) and the remaining family of Rustam. Faramarz, Rustam’s son, is shown here valiantly defending his homeland, but in the end he lost the battle, his land, and his life. The artist Aqa Mirak painted in the mature Safavid style, characterized here by large‑scale figures set close to the foreground. This style anticipates that of illustrated manuscripts and single-figure studies of the 1540s and ’50s.

Several battles follow the death of Qubad, cach one successively worse, Eventually the Iranians flee to a castle, where the Turanians besiege them. In defiance of Shah Nauzar, Qaran, the commander of the Iranian army and Qubad's brother, and a group of like minded troops sneak out of their fortress to rescue their families in Iran. However, they encounter Barman, the murderer of Qubad. Qaran gives chase until he spears the Turanian and beheads him.

en: Folio 127 Rustam Brings the Div King to Kay Kavus for Execution

value.en: Folio 127 Rustam Brings the Div King to Kay Kavus for Execution

Even with the death of the White Div and the Div General Arzhang, the demons ofthe Div King of Mazandaran continue to harry the Iranians. At last, Kay Kavus leadshis army into battle against the dips. Rustam captures the. Div King, who transforms himself into a stone. When Rustam lugs him to Kay Kavus and threatens to slice him up, he returns to div form and is executed on Kay Kavus's order. Later hand blackened the face and hands of Kay Kavus.

en: Folio 127v Rustam Brings the Div King to Kay Kavus for Execution

value.en: Folio 127v Rustam Brings the Div King to Kay Kavus for Execution

Even with the death of the White Div and the Div General Arzhang, the demons ofthe Div King of Mazandaran continue to harry the Iranians.
