After gazing at his newly rediscovered son and recognizing his noble qualities, Sam has him dressed in royal raiment and leads him home in a procession.

Pursued by the potential usurper Bahram Chubina, Shah Khusrau Parviz fled up a narrow gorge. Reaching an impasse, Khusrau prayed to God for help, and instantly the angel Surush appeared and whisked him to safety. The painting is attributed to Muzaffar_Ali, a leading artist of the younger generation and great-nephew of the renowned painter Bihzad. In keeping with his other compositions, the elements appear to fly apart, with rocks jutting in every direction. His brushwork is somewhat painterly, consisting of layers of pale hues rather than the customary technique of painting with richly saturated colors.

en: Folio 84v Sindukht Comes to Sam Bearing Gifts

value.en: Folio 84v Sindukht Comes to Sam Bearing Gifts

Sindukht, in the guise of Mihrab's ambassadress, meets with Sam. The retinue bearing her gift of horses, elephants, silks, gold coins, slaves, and gems stretches tor two miles. Considering Sindukht's offer, Sam decides that the only way to avoid the wrath of Manuchihr or Zal is to entrust the riches to Zal's treasurer, to be held in trust for Rudaba. At this good news Sindukht's companions bring bowls of rubies to pile before Sam.

en: Folio 40v Fariduns Envoy to King Sarv Second Interview

value.en: Folio 40v Fariduns Envoy to King Sarv Second Interview

Sarv's advisers tell him that he cannot avoid marrying his daughters to Faridun's sons without a cunning, plan. Meanwhile, Jandal is summoned again and informed that the proposal will be entertained seriously ihe brings Faridun's three sons from Iran. Jandal kisses Sarv's throne and departs.

en: Folio 339r Planning for the Joust of the Twelve Rooks

value.en: Folio 339r Planning for the Joust of the Twelve Rooks

In reaction to the stalemate between their armies, Gudarz and Piran hold a parlev and agree on a series of eleven jousts to decide the winner of the war. In cach camp the commanders choose the ten champions who, in addition to themselves, will oust. “As a venue, they select a field between two mounds so that the winners can plant their flags on their respective peaks

en: Folio 57v Qaran Captures tbe Castle of the Alans

value.en: Folio 57v Qaran Captures tbe Castle of the Alans

To prevent Salm from sheltering in his impenetrable castle, Manuchihr sends the courtier Qaran, disguised as a Turanian, with the official seal taken from the defeated. Tur. Qaran relieves the gatekeeper and. summons the Iranian army, which storms the castle and burns it to the ground.

en: Folio 66v Sam Takes Leave of Zal

value.en: Folio 66v Sam Takes Leave of Zal

Although Sam and Zal return to Zabulistan, their kingdom in the east, Manuchihr summons Sam to a war in Mazandaran. Sam thus entrusts Zal with the rule of the kingdom with the help of the local elders and wise men.

en: Folio 71v Rudabas Maids Return to the Palace

value.en: Folio 71v Rudabas Maids Return to the Palace

Rudaba's maids hurry back to her palace, where they are chided by a guard for staying out so late. They respond that they have been picking flowers, then regale their mistress with stories of Zal's qualities.

en: Folio 26v The Snakes of King Zahhak

value.en: Folio 26v The Snakes of King Zahhak

Now king of Arabia, Zahhak employs a cook—Iblis in disguise who produces a succession of delicacies that carn Zahhak's praise. For his efforts the cook asks only to kiss the king's shoulders. Upon doing so, the cook disappears, and from Zahhak's shoulders spring, two ravenous snakes whose appetites can be appeased only with human brains.

en: Folio 41v Fariduns Sons with the Daughters of King Sarv

value.en: Folio 41v Fariduns Sons with the Daughters of King Sarv

Atter failing to fool the sons of Faridun about the ages of his daughters, Sarv agrees to the marriages. Sarv then conjures up a nighttime frost to freeze the princes as they sleep, but thev are protected by Faridun's poodness, and the Yemeni king finally concedes to the triple wedding.

en: Folio 343v The Seventh Joust of the Rooks Hajir versus Sipahram

value.en: Folio 343v The Seventh Joust of the Rooks Hajir versus Sipahram

The seventh champion, Hajir, another son of Gudarz, is pitted against Sipahram, a famous Turanian paladin, They draw their swords and battle furiously until Hajir strikes Sipahram's head and he breathes his last. Once again a banner is fixed on the Iranian mound

en: Folio 343v The Seventh Joust of the Rooks Hajir versus Sipahram

value.en: Folio 343v The Seventh Joust of the Rooks Hajir versus Sipahram

The seventh champion, Hajir, another son of Gudarz, is pitted against Sipahram, a famous Turanian paladin, They draw their swords and battle furiously until Hajir strikes Sipahram's head and he breathes his last. Once again a banner is fixed on the Iranian mound

en: Folio 124v Rustam Seventh Courseو He Kills the White Div

value.en: Folio 124v Rustam Seventh Courseو He Kills the White Div

With Aulad's help Rustam finds the White Div's cave but waits until the demon is asleep before proceeding. The div wakes and attacks Rustam, but finally Rustam lifts him up and hurls him to the floor. He cuts out the White Div's liver and, returning to the shah, restores his sight with the demon's blood.

en: Folio 182v Siyavush and Afrasivab in the Hunting Field

value.en: Folio 182v Siyavush and Afrasivab in the Hunting Field

Soon after his polo and archery demonstration, Siyavush joins Afrasiyab and his men at the hunt. Without delay Siyavush slices an onager in two and amasses a huge pile of slaughtered game. His success sets off a chain of grumbling among the Turanian worthies, but Afrasiyab is unaware of their discontent and showers Siyavush with the favor that his brother Garsivaz and son Jahn once enjoyed

As the Iranian and Turanian armies mass on the Plain of Dahistan, neither side makes the first move until. Barman, a young cousin ol Afrasiyab of Turan, charges up to the Iranian front. Only one soldier responds to his challenge to fight. Qubad, chief paladin before Zal and Rustam, scizes his lance and rushes at Barman. After dueling for many hours, Barman prevails and Qubad falls to his death.

en: Folio 127v Rustam Brings the Div King to Kay Kavus for Execution

value.en: Folio 127v Rustam Brings the Div King to Kay Kavus for Execution

Even with the death of the White Div and the Div General Arzhang, the demons ofthe Div King of Mazandaran continue to harry the Iranians. At last, Kay Kavus leadshis army into battle against the dips. Rustam captures the. Div King, who transforms himself into a stone. When Rustam lugs him to Kay Kavus and threatens to slice him up, he returns to div form and is executed on Kay Kavus's order. Later hand blackened the face and hands of Kay Kavus.

en: Folio 25r The Death of King Mirdas

value.en: Folio 25r The Death of King Mirdas
The Death of King Mirdas’, Folio 25 from the Shahnamah of Shah Tahmasp

Now that the war is over, Manuchihr's army gathers all the booty from Rum and Turan and loads it onto elephants before setting out for Iran. Meanwhile, Faridun assembles a host of men blowing horns, beating drums, and driving richly caparisoned elephants to the place where he and Manuchihr meet and embrace.

According to Firdausi, the very idea of kingship originated with King Kayumars, who ruled for thirty years, overseeing a peaceable kingdom in which men wore leopard-skin robes and wild animals grew tame. In this utopia Kayumars had one secret enemy, the evil Ahriman, whose envy drove him to incite his own son, the Black Div, to murder Siyamak, the son of Kayumars. Before the assassination took place, the angel Surush warned Kayumars of Ahriman’s plot.

en: Folio 578v The Khaqan Captive Before Bahram Gur

value.en: Folio 578v The Khaqan Captive Before Bahram Gur

According to his spies the Khaqan of Chin, Iran’s enemy, and his army were camped in a field near Merv. Bahram Gur and his soldiers marched across the breadth of Iran and caught the Khaqan’s army completely off guard. The Khaqan and many of his nobles were captured and the booty divided amongst the troops of Bahram Gur. Here the Khaqan’s captors lead him to Bahram Gur, astride a richly caparisoned horse, while horns blow and the battle ensues in the background. Under the influence of Mir Musavvir, the second supervisor of the Shahnama project, Abu'l Qasim toned down his Turkmen excesses and conformed to a sober decorative style in this illustration.

en: Folio 81v Zal Questions Sams Intentions Regarding the House of Mihrab

value.en: Folio 81v Zal Questions Sams Intentions Regarding the House of Mihrab

As word spreads to Kabul that Sam will soon be waging war against Mihrab, Zal decides to fight to defend Rudaba and her father. He returns to meet with Sam, determined to win him over. After Zal reminds Sam of his broken promise, Sam hatches a plan. He will write a letter for Zal to deliver to Manuchihr. Seeing Zal in person will cause the shah to relent.

en: Folio 98v The First Clash with the Invading Turanians

value.en: Folio 98v The First Clash with the Invading Turanians

With the death of Manuchihr and Sam, the Turanians decide that the chance for victory against their perennial enemies has arrived. They muster a vast army, which is matched by that of Nauzar. Before the war can begin, the advance guard of both sides meet and engage in battle.

en: Folio 595v Sufarais Victory over the Haital

value.en: Folio 595v Sufarais Victory over the Haital

Following the death of Shah Piruz at the hands of the Haital Turks and their leader, Khushnavaz, the shah's young son Prince Balash accedes to the throne under the regency of the paladin Sufaray, who raises an army to avenge the death of Piruz. They march into Transoxiana and waste no time entering the fray against the Turks. When the Turks realize they have lost the battle, they turn and are pursued by Sufaray.

en: Folio 276v Shangul Stirs the Khaqans Council to War on Rustam

value.en: Folio 276v Shangul Stirs the Khaqans Council to War on Rustam

When Piran returns, the khaqan is presiding over a council of the allies. Shangul, the shah of Hind (India), brushes aside Piran's suggestion that they accept Rustam's conditions. Instead he wishes to engage in single combat with Rustam. Meanwhile, Piran and his clan consider Shangul to be foolhardy and prepare to distance themselves from his partisans on the battlefield.

en: Folio 376v Kay Khusrau Defeats the Army of Makran

value.en: Folio 376v Kay Khusrau Defeats the Army of Makran

Afrasiyab's former allies reject him, so he is forced to travel to Gangdizh, a city built by Siyavush on the Sea of Chin, across the desert of Mukran. Kai Khusrau sends envoys to the local king to ask for provisions and safe passage, but the shah of Mukran instead prepares for war. The two sides are at an impasse until Giv rides forth and spears the shah of Mukran with his lance, hastening the end of the war.

en: Folio 135r Rustam and the Seven Champions of Iran Hunt in Turan

value.en: Folio 135r Rustam and the Seven Champions of Iran Hunt in Turan

At a feast with the Seven Champions, Rustam proposes a hunt on Afrasiyab's land. After a week of the Iranians slaughtering all manner of prey, followed by feasting, and wine, Afrasiyab gets wind of the trespassers and gathers a huge army to attack them.

en: Folio 141r Subrab Ropes in His Steed

value.en: Folio 141r Subrab Ropes in His Steed

On a hunting expedition Rustam crosses into Turan in search of Rakhsh, whom he thinks is lost. As a guest in the castle ofa vassal king, Rustam is unexpectedly joined in his bedchamber by the king’s beautiful daughter, Tahmina. ‘The next day, before Rustam departs, they marry, and nine months later Tahmina gives birth to a son, Suhrab. Eventually she tells him the identity of his father, and Suhrab, believing he is bound lor glory, demands a suitable horse. He chooses one from the line of Rakhsh, who had enjoyed equine love the same night as his master.

en: Folio 639v Buzurjmihr Masters the Hindu Game of Chess

value.en: Folio 639v Buzurjmihr Masters the Hindu Game of Chess

In negotiations between Iran and India concerning the payment of tribute to Iran, the king of India sent a challenge to the shah. If the shah could figure out the rules of the game that we know as chess from the board and pieces that he sent with his envoy, India would pay the tribute. If the shah failed, Iran would have to pay tribute to India. The wise vizier Buzurjmihr was summoned and, after a day and night, announced that he had mastered the game; he then proceeded to show his skill against the Indian envoy. This painting depicts Buzurgmihr being challenged at a game of chess.

en: Folio 68v Mihrab Describes Zal to Sindukht and Rudaba

value.en: Folio 68v Mihrab Describes Zal to Sindukht and Rudaba

Returning home, Mihrab visits the harem, where he tells his wite, Sindukht, and daughter, Rudaba, about Zal, describing his strength, fairness, and generosity as well as his white hair. Rudaba blushes to consider this newly discovered prince.

en: Folio 341r The First Joust of the Rooks Fariburz versus Kalbad

value.en: Folio 341r The First Joust of the Rooks Fariburz versus Kalbad

The first rivals are Fariburz, uncle of Kay Khusrau, and Kalbad, brother of Piram When the arrows Fariburz shoots at his foe miss their mark, he draws his sword and slices Kalbad in two, from neck to waist. He then ascends the mound and places the first Iranian flag on it

en: Folio 349v Gustaham Slays Lahhak and Farshidvard

value.en: Folio 349v Gustaham Slays Lahhak and Farshidvard

Although the commanders have agreed that the victors of the jousts will become the masters of the opposing army, Lahhak and Farshidvard decide to flee. In the Iranian camp Gustaham, who has stayed behind to command the army, volunteers to follow them and bring them to justice. The three meet in a plain, and Gustaham releases a volley of arrows, one of which pierces the skull of Farshidvard. Lahhak now shoots an arrow at Gustaham and hits his mark, but the Iranian draws his sword and stabs Lahhak in the throat.

en: Folio 461v Rustam and Isfandiyar Begin Their Combat

value.en: Folio 461v Rustam and Isfandiyar Begin Their Combat

After a lengthy discussion of Gushtasp's demands of Rustam and his and Isfandiyar's obligations to the shah, they decide to resolve the disagreement through combat. Fighting first with lances, they parry and pierce each other's armor until the lances fall apart. They then turn to their swords but again succeed only in breaking each other's weapons. So it goes with maces and hand combat as well, and by the end of the day, neither has the upper hand. They decide to rest.

en: Folio 10r The poet Firdausi reciting from his work before the Ghaznavid sultan Mahmud

value.en: Folio 10r The poet Firdausi reciting from his work before the Ghaznavid sultan Mahmud

The poet Firdausi reciting from his work before the Ghaznavid sultan Mahmud. Folio from the 'Shahnameh of Shah Tahmasp'. Tabriz, c. 1525. Firdausi's presence at the court of Sultan Mahmud and his commission to write the Sbahnama chagrin his jealous rivals, who maintain that only the stories and 71 not Firdausi's poetry are worthy of royal favor. A literary debate ensues in which Firdausi, in the presence of the sultan, composes new verses on the spot that enliven an otherwise dull episode, a feat applauded by Sultan Mahmud.

en: Folio 123r Kai Kavus and Rustam Embrace

value.en: Folio 123r Kai Kavus and Rustam Embrace

Aulad now leads Rustam to the captive Kai Kavus and his contingent, all of whom have been blinded. The shah embraces Rustam, then advises him to find the White Div before he is discovered, to do him in, and to bring his blood to drip into the eyes of the Iranians to restore their sight.

en: Folio 29v Zahhak is Told His Fate

value.en: Folio 29v Zahhak is Told His Fate

The evil king Zahhak faints when his wise men reveal the meaning of his nightmare: that one Faridun, as yet unborn, will justly bring about his downfall and death. The logical disposition of figures in space reflects the influence of the late Timurid Herat School of painting, whereas the bending rosebush and snail-like clouds at the right derive from Turkmen painting as practiced at Tabriz. The animated characterizations, however, are typical of the work of Sultan_Muhammad , head of the royal workshop.

en: Folio 633v Nushirvan Greets the Khaqans Daughter

value.en: Folio 633v Nushirvan Greets the Khaqans Daughter

When the Khaqan of Chin became aware of the greatness and power of the Iranian shah, he determined to form an alliance and offered one of his daughters in marriage to cement it. Nushirvan accepted the offer, but sent a wise envoy to choose the best of the Khaqan’s five daughters. Four were descended from slaves, while the mother of the fifth was a princess. This daughter was the Khaqan’s favorite and he did not wish to lose her. Despite the attempt by the girl’s mother to dress her poorly, her superior breeding and beauty were obvious and soon she left for Iran, where Nushirvan was overcome with astonishment at her loveliness. The distinctive style of Dust_Muhammad, a second-generation Shahnama artist, is evident in the elongated silhouettes of the figures and the flattened profile of the servant in the foreground.

en: Folio 715v Bahram Chubina Slays the Lion-Ape

value.en: Folio 715v Bahram Chubina Slays the Lion-Ape

To thank the Khaqan of Chin for granting him safe haven, Bahram Chubina resolved to rid his host’s land of Kappi the lion-ape, a ferocious creature that had devoured the Khaqan’s daughter. Mir Musavvir delighted in the depiction of the red- and green-eyed monster, rendering every detail of its scaly skin and bloody wounds with fastidious care. The bystanders raise fingers to their lips in astonishment as Bahram Chubina delivers the coup de grace.

en: Folio 72v Rudaba Makes a Ladder of Her Tresses

value.en: Folio 72v Rudaba Makes a Ladder of Her Tresses

One of Rudaba's maids sneaks Zal through the palace to the princess's pavilion. Although Rudaba lowers her tresses for him to scale the wall, Zal tosses up his lasso to catch the crenellation at the roof, then climbs up to meet his beloved.

en: Folio 82v One Blow, Sam Recounts How He Slew a Dragon

value.en: Folio 82v One Blow, Sam Recounts How He Slew a Dragon

In his letter to Manuchihr, Sam reminds the shah of his many battles against dirs in the service of Tran. He retells the story of his battle with the dragon of the River Kashaf, “as large as a mountain.” After shooting arrows into the dragon's maw, Sam bashed its skull with his ox head mace. This teat earned him the name 'One Blow' Sam.

en: Folio 84r Sindukht Comes to Sam Bearing Gifts

value.en: Folio 84r Sindukht Comes to Sam Bearing Gifts

Sindukht, in the guise of Mihrab's ambassadress, meets with Sam. The retinue bearing her gift of horses, elephants, silks, gold coins, slaves, and gems stretches tor two miles. Considering Sindukht's offer, Sam decides that the only way to avoid the wrath of Manuchihr or Zal is to entrust the riches to Zal's treasurer, to be held in trust for Rudaba. At this good news Sindukht's companions bring bowls of rubies to pile before Sam.

en: Folio 85v Sam Seals His Pact with Sindukht

value.en: Folio 85v Sam Seals His Pact with Sindukht

Although Sam is still ignorant of Sindukht's identity, he asks her probing questions about herself and Rudaba. She refuses to answer until Sam has promised to ensure her safety and that of her family, no matter what happens to Kabul. He takes her hand to seal the vow, and she tells him her true identity.

en: Folio 225v Kay Khusraus War Prizes Are Pledged

value.en: Folio 225v Kay Khusraus War Prizes Are Pledged

In preparation for war with the Turanians, Kai Khusrau ordered treasures and armor to be brought forth from the royal treasury and promised as prizes to the winners of six feats of prowess in the coming hostilities. Trays of gems and armor sit on the ground before the king. The style of this painting is characteristic of the third DirectorOfWorkShop of the Shahnama project, Aqa Mirak. The harmony of the composition is achieved not only by balancing solids and voids but also through the distribution of ultramarine blue, orange-red, and light green across the image against a ground of light blue.

en: Folio 268v The Combat of Rustam and Ashkabus

value.en: Folio 268v The Combat of Rustam and Ashkabus

With daylight the battle resumes. Before the armies can clash, Ashkabus rides out from the Turanian side and starts to abuse the Iranians. After Ruhham tries unsuccessfully to shoot Ashkabus with arrows, Rustam enters the fray. He is on foot, having left Rakhsh to rest. He parries the taunts of Ashkabus, then shoots the Turanian's horse with a single arrow. After that, one arrow from Rustam's bow finishes him off.

en: Folio 295r Rustam Recovers Rakhsh from Afrasiyabs Herd

value.en: Folio 295r Rustam Recovers Rakhsh from Afrasiyabs Herd

Rustam chooses to be hurled at the mountain, knowing the Div will toss him into the sea, By the time he returns to the spring, Rakhsh is missing. Rustam follows his tracks and eventually spots Rakhsh in a pleasant valley amid a herd of horses. This is in fact Alrasiyab herd. When Rustam ropes Rakhsh and rides off toward Iran, the whole herd follows, but they are pursued lirst by the royal Turanian guard and then by Afrasiyab and his paladins.

en: Folio 346r The Final Joust of the Rooks Gudarz Versus Piran

value.en: Folio 346r The Final Joust of the Rooks Gudarz Versus Piran

The artist has captured the essentials of this tragic story with delicate sensitivity. The Iranian Gudarz has wounded the old Turanian warrior Piran, but in respect for his seasoned enemy, has offered him safe passage to the Turanian army. Piran, seen through a gap in the rocks, refuses this, preferring the inevitable warrior’s death to surrender. The grooms in the foreground frame the scene, in which the poses of the two adversaries—Gudarz in pursuit, Piran skulking up the mountain—give visual expression to the essential dilemma of the story.

en: Folio 39v Faridun Envoy to King Sarv First Interview

value.en: Folio 39v Faridun Envoy to King Sarv First Interview

The daughters of Jamshid bear Faridun three sons, whom he loves so much that he refuses to name them. When thev reach maturity, he sends his adviser, Jandal, to find for them three wives who also have remained unnamed because of their father's love. Jandal finds three princesses in Yemen, the daughters of King Sarv, but. Sarv is loath to part with his cherished ones,

Faridun's joy at his son's home coming, turns to sorrow when he sees the head of Iraj. In his gricf he rips his clothes and tears out his hair. Placing Iraj's head in his lap, he prays to live long enough to see a descendant of Iraj avenge his death

en: Folio 130r The Marriage of Sudaba and Kai Kavus

value.en: Folio 130r The Marriage of Sudaba and Kai Kavus

After defeating the king of Hamavaran, Kai Kavus demands his daughter Sudaba's hand in marriage. Her beauty astonishes him, and his advisers agree that she is a perfect match for him. They marry and begin a weeklong celebration. However, Sudaba's father then lures Kai Kavus to his palace and, after eight days of revelry, takes him and his men prisoner.

en: Folio 56v Faridun Receives the Head of Tur

value.en: Folio 56v Faridun Receives the Head of Tur
Manuchihr dispatches a camel bearing the head of Tur so that Faridun can learn of his victory without delay, Although the loss of his son is bittersweet, Faridun praises Manuchihr.

en: Folio 568r Bahram Gur Pins the Coupling Onagers

value.en: Folio 568r Bahram Gur Pins the Coupling Onagers

One day, Shah Bahram and a large party of horsemen were hunting in a plain overrun with onagers, or wild asses. Letting fly an arrow at a buck mounting his mate, Bahram pinned them together and earned the nickname Bahram Gur, or "Onagers" Bahram. Distinguished for his meticulous attention to detail, Mir Sayyid 'Ali had been raised in the Safavid artists’ atelier under the tutelage of his father, Mir Musavvir, the second director of Shah Tahmasp’s Shahnama project. The simurgh on Bahram Gur’s saddlecloth and intricate designs on his quiver and saddle are typical of Mir Sayyid Ali’s work

en: Folio 342r The Fourth Joust of the Rooks Faruhil versus Zangula

value.en: Folio 342r The Fourth Joust of the Rooks Faruhil versus Zangula

The fourth joust features Faruhil, an Iranian champion known for his skill with the bow, against the Turanian Zangula. After peppering Zangula with arrows, Karuhil shoots one that hits both Zangula and his horse, which falls forward, throwing its rider. Zangula falls on his head and dies instantly

en: Folio 118r: Rustam First Course: Rakhsb Slays a Lion

value.en: Folio 118r: Rustam First Course: Rakhsb Slays a Lion

After ruling for one hundred years, Kay Qubad dies and is succeeded by his son Kay Kavus, a foolhardy youth. Kay Kavus sets off on an expedition to Mazandaran, home of divs who eventually capture and blind him. A message reaches Rustam, and he embarks on a rescue mission on the shorter but more dangerous route—the scene of his seven "courses" or challenges. After two days riding, Rustam lies down to sleep in a meadow, not realizing it is a lion's den. The lion returns and attacks the horse Rakhsh, who crunches the lions neck with his teeth and tramples it. Rustam wakes and chides his horse for taking on the lion.

en: Folio 163v Siyavush Stands Accused by Sudaba before Kai Kavus

value.en: Folio 163v Siyavush Stands Accused by Sudaba before Kai Kavus

Kai Kavus marries a second wife, a Turanian princess, who gives birth to a son, Siyavush. Because the boy has an ill-omened horoscope, Kai Kavus sends him to be reared by Rustam in Zabulistan, but when he comes of age, he returns to his father's court. Soon afterward his mother dies, and Kai Kavus recommends that Siyavush seek solace with his first wife, Sudaba. She falls in love with Siyavush, and when he spurns her advances, she claims that he tried to rape her. Kai Kavus questions his wife and son and determines the innocence of Siyavush, but decides the matter should be kept secret.

en: Folio 36v Faridun strikes Zahhak with the ox-headed mace

value.en: Folio 36v: Faridun strikes Zahhak with the ox-headed mace

Kai Kavus marries a second wife, a Turanian princess, who gives birth to a son, Siyavush. Because the boy has an ill-omened horoscope, Kai Kavus sends him to be reared by Rustam in Zabulistan, but when he comes of age, he returns to his father's court. Soon afterward his mother dies, and Kai Kavus recommends that Siyavush seek solace with his first wife, Sudaba. She falls in love with Siyavush, and when he spurns her advances, she claims that he tried to rape her. Kai Kavus questions his wife and son and determines the innocence of Siyavush, but decides the matter should be kept secret.

en: Folio 155r Rustam Weeps over His Dying Son The Third Day

value.en: Folio 155r Rustam Weeps over His Dying Son The Third Day

On the third day Suhrab and Rustam wrestle again, butthistime Rustam throws the vouth to the ground and stabs him. Ashe lies dving, Suhrab says that he had come secking Rustam but now someone else had killed him. Rustam then identifies himself and, tearing otf Suhrab's coat of mail, finds the onyx armband he had given Tahmina the night of their marriage. Understanding, fully the tragedy of his deed, Rustam weeps bitterly.

en: Folio 342r The Fourth Joust of the Rooks Faruhil versus Zangula

value.en: Folio 342r The Fourth Joust of the Rooks Faruhil versus Zangula

The fourth joust features Faruhil, an Iranian champion known for his skill with the bow, against the Turanian Zangula. After peppering Zangula with arrows, Karuhil shoots one that hits both Zangula and his horse, which falls forward, throwing its rider. Zangula falls on his head and dies instantly

en: Folio 62v Zal Is Sighted by a Caravan

value.en: Folio 62v Zal Is Sighted by a Caravan

Hearing Zal's cries in the wilderness, a magical simurgh scoops him up in her claws and carries him to her nest high in the mountains, where she raises him with her own young. On occasion a caravan passes, and travelers notice Zal in the simurgh's nest

en: Folio 435v Isfandiyar's Fourth Course, He Slays a Sorceress

value.en: Folio 435v Isfandiyar's Fourth Course, He Slays a Sorceress

Hearing Zal's cries in the wilderness, a magical simurgh scoops him up in her claws and carries him to her nest high in the mountains, where she raises him with her own young. On occasion a caravan passes, and travelers notice Zal in the simurgh's nest

en: Folio 185v The Wedding of Siyavush and Farangis

value.en: Folio 185v: The Wedding of Siyavush and Farangis

One day, Shah Bahram and a large party of horsemen were hunting in a plain overrun with onagers, or wild asses. Letting fly an arrow at a buck mounting his mate, Bahram pinned them together and earned the nickname Bahram Gur, or "Onagers" Bahram. Distinguished for his meticulous attention to detail, Mir Sayyid 'Ali had been raised in the Safavid artists’ atelier under the tutelage of his father, Mir Musavvir, the second director of Shah Tahmasp’s Shahnama project. The simurgh on Bahram Gur’s saddlecloth and intricate designs on his quiver and saddle are typical of Mir Sayyid 'Ali’s work

Nushirvan (r. 531-79) became known for the reforms he instituted, especially in the system of taxation. Here he dictates to a scribe a letter to his agents, outlining the new system and also setting forth principles of conduct: judiciousness and absolute scrupulousness in the administration of their duties. Muzaffar 'Ali, who painted this illustration, worked for the Safavid court through the entire reign of Shah Tahmasp. As is typical of works by the second generation of Shahnama artists, the figures here are larger in scale and fewer in number than in comparable Shahnama compositions completed between 1525 and 1530.

en: Folio 51v Faridun and Manuchihr Receive an Envoy from Salm and Tur

value.en: Folio 51v Faridun and Manuchihr Receive an Envoy from Salm and Tur

Receiving word of Manuchihi's expanded role, Salm and ‘Tur send an envoy laden with gifts to seck Faridun's forgiveness and ask Manuchihr to visit so they can serve him. From his throne with Manuchihr at his side, Faridun rejects this overture and vows to avenge Iraj's death with the brothers blood

After more coaxing, Sindukht convinces Mihrab to see Rudaba, whom she has advised to come weeping to her father dressed in her finest raiment. Although he is stunned by her beauty, Mihrab sternly chides Rudaba tor her choice.

en: Folio 592r Yazdigird II Accedes to the Throne

value.en: Folio 592r Yazdigird II Accedes to the Throne

One day, Shah Bahram and a large party of horsemen were hunting in a plain overrun with onagers, or wild asses. Letting fly an arrow at a buck mounting his mate, Bahram pinned them together and earned the nickname Bahram Gur, or "Onagers" Bahram. Distinguished for his meticulous attention to detail, Mir Sayyid 'Ali had been raised in the Safavid artists’ atelier under the tutelage of his father, Mir Musavvir, the second director of Shah Tahmasp’s Shahnama project. The simurgh on Bahram Gur’s saddlecloth and intricate designs on his quiver and saddle are typical of Mir Sayyid 'Ali’s work

In another confrontation Rustam and Rakhsh are injured and retreat. Now Zal intervenes and summons the simurgh, his foster mother, who provides healing feathers and a special arrow. Once again Rustam and Isfandiyar meet in single combat, but thls time, though Isfandiyar's arrows miss their mark, Rustam's pierce the prince's eyes. As Isfandiyar lies dying, both he and Rustam know that this murder, no matter how it was provoked, will bring trouble down on Rustam's head.

This richly ornamented rosette, like a sunburst in the manuscript, is inscribed. In the upper cartouche one reads: In His Name, the Most Praised and Most Exalted! IN THE ROSETTE: Commissioned for the Library of the most mighty Sultan, and the most just and beneficent Khaqan [Grand Khan], sultan, son and grandson of sultans, Abul-Muzaffar [The Victorious], Sultan Shah Tahmasp, of Huseyni and Safavid descent, Bahadur [The Valiant] Khan. May God, the Most Exalted, perpetuate his realm and his rule, and diffuse . . . IN THE LOWER CARTOUCHE: ... his justice and his benevolence throughout the world!
Tur responds by drawing his dagger and stabbing Iraj. He then beheads his young brother and sends the head wrapped in scented cloth to Faridun.

While Afrasiyab fights at Dahistan, a supplementary force is detailed to attack Zabul, home of Zal, of which Mihrab has been left in charge. Through bribery and persuasion, he keeps the Turanians at bay. An urgent message reaches Zal, who returns immediately with his army. He soon dispatches Khazarvan, one of the Turanian commanders, by smashing him on the head with his ox-head mace, then trampling him to death.

en: Folio 182v Siyavush and Afrasivab in the Hunting Field

value.en: Folio 182v Siyavush and Afrasivab in the Hunting Field

Soon after his polo and archery demonstration, Siyavush joins Afrasiyab and his men at the hunt. Without delay Siyavush slices an onager in two and amasses a huge pile of slaughtered game. His success sets off a chain of grumbling among the Turanian worthies, but Afrasiyab is unaware of their discontent and showers Siyavush with the favor that his brother Garsivaz and son Jahn once enjoyed

en: Folio 119v Rustams Third Course He Slays a Dragon

value.en: Folio 119v Rustams Third Course He Slays a Dragon

Atter crossing a desert and finding, water and game, Rustam again lies down to sleep. This time a dragon appears, visible only to Rakhsh. Twice the horse whinnies to warn his master, and twice Rustam berates Rakhsh for waking, him. The third time, the dragon roars, and Rakhsh and Rustam attack, biting the beast's neck and slicing off its head, respectively.

en: Folio 120v Rustams Fourth Course, He Cleaves a Witch

value.en: Folio 120v Rustams Fourth Course, He Cleaves a Witch

The hero Rustam underwent seven tests of valor on his way to rescue Shah Kai Kavus. The artist has illustrated the climax of Rustam’s fourth course when the beautiful maiden who had beguiled him turned back into the witch she really was, to be dispatched by his sword. The sorcerer in the background, raising his hands in horror, has been borrowed from Turkmen artistic sources, while the profile of the sorceress is an exaggerated version of the conventional one for the old retainer or crone. Note the faces of animals and humans incorporated in the rocks, a favorite element of the early paintings of Tahmasp’s Shahnama.

en: Folio 135v Rustam and the Seven Champions of Iran Hunt in Turan

value.en: Folio 135v Rustam and the Seven Champions of Iran Hunt in Turan

At a feast with the Seven Champions, Rustam proposes a hunt on Afrasiyab's land. After a week of the Iranians slaughtering all manner of prey, followed by feasting, and wine, Afrasiyab gets wind of the trespassers and gathers a huge army to attack them.

Tahmuras, shown here galloping across a meadow, defeated the divs (demons); in exchange for their lives, they taught him the art of writing. This work is attributed to Sultan_Muhammad , the master painter and chief administrator of the first generation of artists of this manuscript

en: Folio 265v Combat of Giv and Kamus

value.en: Folio 265v Combat of Giv and Kamus
This painting depicts the combat between the Iranian hero Giv and Kamus of Kashan. It shows the moment when, surrounded by their opposing armies, Giv’s lance was “cleaved obliquely like a pen.

Hushang succeeds Keyumars and presides over the development of metalworking and animal husbandry. When he throws a rock, striking a boulder, and sparks turn to flame, he recognizes fire as a divine gilt and initiates the feast of Sada to celebrate this discovery.

en: Folio 35r The Feast of Faridun and Kundrau

value.en: Folio 35r The Feast of Faridun and Kundrau
Faridun prevails upon Kundrau to organize a victory feast, but Kundrau flees to join Zahhak. He is gone by morning

en: Folio 708r The Angel Surush Rescues Khusrau Parviz from a Cul-de-sac

value.en: Folio 708r The Angel Surush Rescues Khusrau Parviz from a Cul-de-sac

Pursued by the potential usurper Bahram Chubina, Shah Khusrau Parviz fled up a narrow gorge. Reaching an impasse, Khusrau prayed to God for help, and instantly the angel Surush appeared and whisked him to safety. The painting is attributed to Muzaffar_Ali, a leading artist of the younger generation and great-nephew of the renowned painter Bihzad. In keeping with his other compositions, the elements appear to fly apart, with rocks jutting in every direction. His brushwork is somewhat painterly, consisting of layers of pale hues rather than the customary technique of painting with richly saturated colors.

en: Folio 79v Sam Is Visited by Prince Nauzar

value.en: Folio 79v Sam Is Visited by Prince Nauzar

When word reaches Manuchihr that Sam and Mihrab have consented to their children's marriage, he summons the magi, but they simply note his superior wisdom. He then sends Prince Nauzar to bring Sam back to his court. First Sam and Nauzar enjoy a feast in Sam's encampment.

en: Folio 204v Surkha Captured by Faramarz is Condemned by Rustam

value.en: Folio 204v Surkha Captured by Faramarz is Condemned by Rustam

The hero Rustam, shown here on horseback wearing a leopard-skin hat, launched war to avenge the death of Siyavush, whom the Turanian king Afrasiyab had executed. Rustam was determined to cause Afrasiyab the same pain by having his son, Surkha, captured and killed. The Chinese‑style kite clouds, winding tree, and doll‑like rendition of the figures’ faces reflect Qasim ibn 'Ali’s grounding in the Turkmen painting tradition.

en: Folio 70v Rudaba s Maids Meet Zals Page at the River

value.en: Folio 70v Rudaba s Maids Meet Zals Page at the River

Rudaba's maids go to the riverbank near Zal's camp. Zal sends his page to collect a bird he has shot, which lies dead near the girls, but also to determine why they arc there. They explain their mission to the page, who informs Zal in due course.

en: Folio 77v Mihrab Hears of Rudaba Folly

value.en: Folio 77v Mihrab Hears of Rudaba Folly

Mihrab comes to Sindukht's chamber, where she speaks poetically of death and the passage of time. She then tells him that Rudaba has lost her heart to Zal, prompting Mihrab to threaten to kill his daughter. Sindukht manages to calm him, but Mihrab worries that Manuchihr will never consent to this marriage.

en: Folio 707v Khusrau Parvizs Charge against Bahram Chubina

value.en: Folio 707v Khusrau Parvizs Charge against Bahram Chubina

Eventually a large army from Rum joins Khusrau Parviz and his Iranians and marches toward Azarbaijan. After a Rumi hero dies on the first day of battle, Khusrau decides to take a small band of Iranians with him the next day. Although Bahram Chubina enters the fray with only three soldiers, he puts Khusrau and his men to flight.

In this painting, Zal is depicted enthroned as he consults his magi, or Zoroastrian wise men, on how to win his father’s approval to marry Rudaba, the granddaughter of the evil king Zahhak. The phoenix-like bird on the canopy may refer to the simurgh, which saved and nurtured the infant Zal, whose parents had rejected him for being an albino. The sinuous flowering trees often appear in Turkmen painting of Tabriz.

en: Folio 202v Rustam Blames Kai Kavus for the Death of Siyavush

value.en: Folio 202v Rustam Blames Kai Kavus for the Death of Siyavush

The news of the death of Siyavush causes lamentation at the Iranian court and in Zabulistan, where Rustam swears to avenge his murder. After a period of mourning, Rustam journeys with his army to the court of Kai Kavus. First, he reproaches the shah for listening to Sudaba and allowing Siyavush to go to Turan. Next, he drags Sudaba out of her chambers and stabs her to death. After seven days of mourning, he issues the call to battle and departs with the army for Turan.

en: Folio 210v Kay Khusrau is Discovered by Giv

value.en: Folio 210v Kay Khusrau is Discovered by Giv

After a seven‑year search for the future shah, the persistent Iranian knight Giv finally discovered the prince Kai Khusrau. Faithful to the story, the painting features an idyllic spring landscape, the remoteness of which is indicated by the barren hill in the background. The black pool next to Kai Khusrau and the stream flowing from it would have originally been silver, which has now tarnished.

en: Folio 299r Bizhan Slaughters the Wild Boars of Irman

value.en: Folio 299r Bizhan Slaughters the Wild Boars of Irman

In the remote land of Irman, a herd of enormous wild boars is destroying, fields and flocks. A delegation seeks the help of Kay Khusrau, who offers a reward to the paladin who can rid Irman of this scourge. Young Bizhan eagerly takes up the challenge and departs with Gurgin as his guide. Singlehandedly he pursues the boars in the forest, slicing in two the head of one and intimidating the rest. Thus, Bizhan easily puts them to the sword.

en: Folio 65v Zal before Manuchihr, Sam, and Qaran

value.en: Folio 65v Zal before Manuchihr, Sam, and Qaran

When Manuchihr hears of the reunion of Sam and Zal, he invites them to court. Enthroned next to his courtier Qaran and son Nauzar, Manuchihr observes Zal in wonder and understands that Zal will become a great paladin.

en: Folio 69v Rudaba Confides in Her Maids

value.en: Folio 69v Rudaba Confides in Her Maids

Unable to keep her passion to herself, Rudaba confesses her love for Zal to her Turkish maids. At first they react negatively, wondering how she can love a white haired youth reared by a bird. However, secing, heranger at their response, they determine to help her meet Zal

en: Folio 745v The Coup against Usurper Shah

value.en: Folio 745v The Coup against Usurper Shah

In the year 630, during a fifty-day reign, Shah Farain Guraz completely dissipated the entire royal treasury. Because of this greed and injustice, the army soon turned against the shah and swore to rid Iran of the usurper. One day while on a hunt, the leader of the coup, Shahran Guraz, drew his bow as if to shoot a stag but instead shot the monarch in the back. The accompanying warriors then began to battle with each other. This illustration appears to take place after the murder of the shah. Unlike many battle scenes, the artist Dust_Muhammad has rendered each horse’s armor with great care, resulting in a rhythmic and colorful pattern across the field of the painting.

After noticing the comings and goings of the woman who delivers the message that Sam consents to Zal and. Ruclaba's marriage, Sindukht falls into a fury, expecting the worst of her daughter. Then Rudaba confesses her love for Zal and announces that Sam has agreed to their union. Sindukht relents and begins plotting how to tell Mihrab and Mauuchihr.

en: Folio 7r Firdausi Encounters the Court Poets of Ghazna

value.en: Folio 7r Firdausi Encounters the Court Poets of Ghazna
On his way to seek the patronage of Sultan Mahmud of Ghazna for the Shabana, Firdausi encounters three of the sultan's poets, who test his poctic skills before allowing him to join them

en: Folio 180v Siyavush Plays Polo before Afrasiyab

value.en: Folio 180v Siyavush Plays Polo before Afrasiyab

The Turanian king Afrasiyab suggested a game of polo and Siyavush accepted, requesting, however, that he be allowed Iranian players for his team, as Turanians would not play their hardest against their own king. Afrasiyab agreed and was wonderfully impressed with the outstanding skill of Siyavush as a player and horseman. Like the other Iranians, Siyavush wears a tall baton, or taj, in his turban. He is the player on the black horse in the center of the field; Afrasiyab, mounted, watches from the horizon.

en: Folio 27v Zahhak Receives the Daughters of Jamshid

value.en: Folio 27v Zahhak Receives the Daughters of Jamshid
As Jamshid becomes increasingly prideful, Iran declines. Zahhak invades and kills Jamshid. During his thousand-year reign of terror, Zahhak takes Jamshid's two daughters as his wives.

en: Folio 434v Isfandiyars Third Course He Slays a Dragon

value.en: Folio 434v Isfandiyars Third Course He Slays a Dragon

This dramatic image illustrates the third of seven challenges, or courses, that Prince Isfandiyar underwent en route to freeing his sisters from captivity in Turan. Learning that he would encounter a dragon on his perilous path, Isfandiyar ordered a horse-drawn cart with a box in which he could hide and from which spears projected. Here, the dragon has appeared and is sucking the horses into its maw, soon to be impaled on the spears and slashed by Isfandiyar’s sword. The tightly coiled dragon slithering through the rocks and breathing fire lends drama and menace to the scene, which otherwise contains many elements associated with Turkmen painting, such as the writhing bare bushes and squat figures, competently rendered by Qasim ibn 'Ali, an artist from Shiraz who was active at the Safavid court in the second quarter of the sixteenth century.

en: Folio 308v Kay Khusran Fetes Rustam beneath the Jewel Tree

value.en: Folio 308v Kay Khusran Fetes Rustam beneath the Jewel Tree

Enraged by the abduction of his daughter and his defeat in the war to avenge Sivavush, Atrasiyab resolves again to wage war on Iran. Ranged on two mountains, the armies face off without fighting tor seven days, but on the eighth day Human rides across the plain and challenges the Iranians. Bizhan sallies forth, and the two decide to duel at dawn. They fight throughout the dav until Bizhan gains the advantage and beheads his foe.

en: Folio 124r Rustam Seventh Courseو He Kills the White Div

value.en: Folio 124r Rustam Seventh Courseو He Kills the White Div

With Aulad's help Rustam finds the White Div's cave but waits until the demon is asleep before proceeding. The dz» wakes and attacks Rustam, but finally Rustam lifts him up and hurls him to the floor. He cuts out the White Div's liver and, returning to the shah, restores his sight with the demon's blood.

en: Folio 742v The Assassination of Khusrau Parviz

value.en: Folio 742v The Assassination of Khusrau Parviz

After many years of honest rule, Khusrau Parviz became unjust and his chiefs plotted to overthrow him. The conspirators placed his eldest son, Shuriya, on the throne and imprisoned Khusrau with his beloved Shirin. However, the power of the deposed shah was still feared and Shuriya’s advisors pressed him to order his father’s assassination. A foulsmelling killer was found and soon he entered Khusrau’s chamber and stabbed him through the heart. This work is one of the few by the young artist Abd_al_Samad, who left for India in the 1540s and was instrumental in founding the Mughal school of painting.

It has been argued that “The Death of Zahhak” dates to around 1535 and is Sultan_Muhammad ’s last contribution to the Shahnameh (Book of Kings) of Shah Tahmasp (folio 37 verso). By the mid-1530s, the style of Safavid court painting had matured, and some of the hallmarks of Sultan_Muhammad ’s own idiom had evolved.

en: Folio 543r Ceasar Captive Before Shapur II

value.en: Folio 543r Ceasar Captive Before Shapur II

Shah Shapur went to Rum disguised as a merchant, but was recognized at Caesar’s court and thrown into prison. Caesar then invaded Iran, causing havoc. A sympathetic young man helped Shapur to escape to Iran, where he gathered an army to take revenge. He captured Caesar but spared his life, castigating him for his evil actions. Aqa Mirak, the last director of the Shahnama project, has minimized the number of figures to heighten the dramatic tension between the protagonists

en: Folio 551v Bahrum Gur Before His Father, Yazdigird I

value.en: Folio 551v Bahrum Gur Before His Father, Yazdigird I

Munzir, Bahram's Arab host, sends a painting of him to his father,Yazdigird I, who is so struck by his son's handsome physique and hunting prowess that he asks Munzir to send him to Iran. Bahram arrives and greatly impresses his father with his manly appearance and proper demeanor. Yazdigird then calls Bahram into his service night and day, a situation that eventually causes Bahram to return to Arabia.

en: Folio 89v Sam and Zal Welcomed into Kabul

value.en: Folio 89v Sam and Zal Welcomed into Kabul

Zal returns to Zabulistan with news of Manuchihr's permission for him to wed. He and Sam proceed toward Kabul and are met on the wav by Mihrab with a large retinue. At Mihrab's palace Sindukht greets the men along with three hundred servants holding cups of gold filled with gems and incense.

King Jamshid, who ruled for three hundred years, ushers in the first golden age in Iran’s history. He introduces to his people the sciences, medicine, and the arts and crafts, and he establishes Now-ruz (New Day) on the first of Farvardin (21 March), when he finally rests from his endeavors. Soon, however, Jamshid becomes increasingly arrogant about his achievements and considers himself better than everyone, even God. His hubris eventually causes him to lose his divine glory (farr), an essential attribute of Persian kingship, and he is killed by the evil Zahhak

Attributed to Aqa Mirak, this painting displays a mastery of pictorial space.[1] The arc of the dragon on the left is offset by the placement of the three figures on horseback, guiding viewers through the painting from the lower centre to the upper right. In this way, viewers not only focus on the primary encounter between rider and dragon in the painting’s foreground, but also explore the reactions of the other two riders. The differences among them are crucial. At the sight of the dragon the oldest son turns and flees, exclaiming that no sane person would fight a dragon. The middle son prepares to fight the beast as if he were in battle with another man. The youngest son stands his ground, telling the dragon to be off because he and his brothers are the sons of Faridun and warriors like their father. Faridun calls the first one Salm because he had sought safety; the second he names Tur for his brash fearlessness; and the third, who astutely and bravely confronted the dragon, is dubbed Iraj. To each Faridun assigns one third of his kingdom. Salm receives Rum, the western provinces; Tur receives the eastern section, Turan; and Iraj is given the central kingdom, Iran.

en: Folio 111v Rustam Spits Qalun on His Own Spear

value.en: Folio 111v Rustam Spits Qalun on His Own Spear

Traveling tojoin Zal and the Iranian army, Rustam and Kay Qubad encounter a band of Turanian soldiers. Qalun, a Turanian officer, launches himselfat Rustam, who charges forward. Qalun then sticks Rustam with his lance, but Rustam jerks it out of his hand and spears him. He lifts Qalun and sticks the lance in the ground, with Qalun impaled as ion a spear.

en: Folio 245r Bahram Recovers the Crown of Rivniz

value.en: Folio 245r Bahram Recovers the Crown of Rivniz

As the battle of Pashan continues, the Iranians start to gain control, but then Prince Rivniz, one of the sons of Kai Kavus, is killed, and his crown falls to the ground of the battlefield. Both the Iranians and the Turanians turn their focus to retrieving the crown until Bahram, son of Gudarz, rushes into the fray and scoops up the crown with the tip of his lance. Although both sides suffer many losses, the Turanians finish victorious

en: Folio 46v Iraj Offers to Visit His Brothers

value.en: Folio 46v Iraj Offers to Visit His Brothers

In response to his brothers threat, Iraj undertakes to visit them, without an army, to express his desire to live in peace. Faridun gives him a letter to carry to his brothers in which the shah reiterates his own peaceable intention.

en: Folio 47v Iraj Begs Tur for mercy

value.en: Folio 47v Iraj Begs Tur for mercy

Iraj's demonstration of conciliation only enrages his brothers. When he reaches their tent, Tur jumps up from his golden seat, scizes it, and brings it crashing down on the head of lraj who beseeches his brothers to spare his lite, keeping God and their familial bonds in mind.

en: Folio 92v Rustam Slays the White Elephant

value.en: Folio 92v Rustam Slays the White Elephant

Nine months alter marrying Zal, Rudaba gives birth by Caesarean section to an enormous son named Rustam. As a boy, Rustam is awakened by a commotion and learns that the king's white elephant is running amok. He grabs Sam's mace, throws the gatekeepers aside, and smashes the elephant on the head, stopping it in its tracks

en: Folio 52v The Envoy Returns to Salm and Tur

value.en: Folio 52v The Envoy Returns to Salm and Tur
The envoy speeds back to Salm and Tur's encampment, terrified by the words of Faridun and fearful of having to relate Faridun's response to his masters.

en: Folio 32v Faridun Orders the Ox-Head Mace

value.en: Folio 32v Faridun Orders the Ox-Head Mace
At age sixteen. Faridun emerges from hiding. First, he parlevs with his two brothers, then orders an ox head mace to be fashioned, so he can lead an army against Zahhak.

en: Folio 622r Buzurjmihr Appears at Nushirvans Fifth Assembly

value.en: Folio 622r Buzurjmihr Appears at Nushirvans Fifth Assembly

Shah Nushirvan became impressed with Buzurgmihr’s insights when he first encountered the sage as a child. By the time he was old enough to participate in a series of assemblies of wise men, such as the scene here, Nushirvan depended on Buzurgmihr more than any other counselor. Here, Muzaffar 'Ali, a versatile painter from the younger generation at the court workshop, has chosen a traditional composition, with the throne dominating the center and the figures symmetrically arranged.

en: Folio 708v The Angel Surush Rescues Khusrau Parviz

value.en: Folio 708v The Angel Surush Rescues Khusrau Parviz
Claiming that his compatriots had spurned Siyavush, Piran offers him his daughter Jarira in marriage. Elegantly clothed and perfumed, she is presented to the young prince, who weds her happily.

en: Folio 404r Gushtasp Proves His Archery before Caesar

value.en: Folio 404r Gushtasp Proves His Archery before Caesar

Next, Gushtasp joins the Rumi champions in an archery contest, and his skill catches the attention of the caesar. In a meeting with the caesar, Gushtasp identifies himself and admits that he has performed the feats of valor on behalf of his brothers-in-law. The caesar forgives him and invites him and his wife back to the palace. Privately, however, he worries about Gushtasp's mysterious origins.

en: Folio 45v Faridun Replies to the Threat of Salm and Tur

value.en: Folio 45v Faridun Replies to the Threat of Salm and Tur
The message from Salm and Tur dismays Faridun, who warns them not to turn to violence.

Sam, Manuchihr's former guardian, becomes a father long after the first blush of youth has passed. The child, Zal, is an albino, thought to be a bad omen, and none of the women of Sam's house will allow him to see the child for a week. When he finally is shown the baby, Sam orders him to be carried off to Mount Alburz and left to die.

en: Folio 33v Faridun Crosses the river Dijla

value.en: Folio 33v Faridun Crosses the river Dijla

Ox-head mace in hand, Faridun leads his army to the banks of the River Dijla (modern Tigris). When the boatmen refuse to ferry the army across the river, Faridun and his horse plunge in, followed by his men, and ford the river.

en: Folio 241r The Besotted Iranian Camp Attacked by Night

value.en: Folio 241r The Besotted Iranian Camp Attacked by Night
Night attack scene from Turano-Iranian wars.

en: Folio 53v Salm and Tur Receive the Reply of Faridun and Manuchihr

value.en: Folio 53v Salm and Tur Receive the Reply of Faridun and Manuchihr
Blanching at the envoys news of Faridun's message and of the regal bearing of Manuchihr, Salm and Tur resolve to muster their army and prepare tor war immediately.

en: Folio 55v Manuchihr Raises Tur on His Lance

value.en: Folio 55v Manuchihr Raises Tur on His Lance

Several skirmishes ensuc, and then Tur and Salm launch a night attack. Manuchihr's scouts learn of the plan and counter the strike, forcing Tur to flee. However Manuchihr catches him using his sword to lift him from his saddle betore beheading him.

en: Folio 586r Bahram Gur Slays the Rhino-Wolf

value.en: Folio 586r Bahram Gur Slays the Rhino-Wolf

Shah Shangul asked Bahram to rid the world of a monstrous rhino‑wolf, which tore the hearts from lions and the skin from leopards. Bahram strung up his bow and sped toward the rhino‑wolf, pouring a mighty hail of arrows onto the beast. In this painting, the image of the rhino‑wolf breaks through the rulings of the painting into the margins, lending the composition dynamism and suggesting the extension of the setting beyond the confines of the page.

en: Folio 121v Rustams Fifth Course, The Capture of Aulad

value.en: Folio 121v Rustams Fifth Course, The Capture of Aulad

Rustam lies down to sleep in a meadow and releases Rakhsh to graze in a field of grain. A watchman interrupts his nap, complaining about the horse in his master's field. Rustam attacks him, and when the owner's other attendants come to help, he slays them, The master, Aulad, is roped and bound and told to lead Rustam to the White Div, who holds Kay Kavus captive in Mazandaran.

After a reign of five hundred years, Faridun abdicates in favor of Manuchihr. The old king dies, and after a period of mourning, Manuchihr assumes the throne, crowns himself, and announces his adherence to the beliets and principles of Faridun.
Near the end of his reign, Zahhak wakes screaming, having dreamed that a warrior with an ox-head mace dragged him, bound, to Mount Damavand.

en: Folio 212r Kay Khusrau Rides Bihzad for the First Time

value.en: Folio 212r Kay Khusrau Rides Bihzad for the First Time

Giv and Kai Khusrau travel to Siyavushgird to tell Farangis their plans. They first need to find Bihzad, Siyavush's mount, who has been allowed to run wild in the mountains since his master's death. When they find Bihzad, the horse allows Kai Khusrau, who is carrying his old saddle, to put it on and mount. The two take off, joined eventually by Giv, and they all return to prepare for their journey.

en: Folio 538r The Coronation of the Infant Shapur II

value.en: Folio 538r The Coronation of the Infant Shapur II

The last section of the Shahnama chronicles the reigns of the Sasanian kings of Iran. Here, the tiny infant Shapur, born after the death of his father Hurmuzd II, is depicted seated on an ornately bejeweled throne as his nurse fans him. The artist Muzaffar 'Ali’s lifelong familiarity with the court and his good-natured approach to its denizens inform the portrayal of the servants bearing jewels and gifts for the new king, the chatting courtiers in the foreground, and the elegant ladies who observe from a window above the door.

Before his death Manuchihr advises his son Nauzar that trouble lies ahead in the form of threats from the Turanians and that Sam, Zal, and Rustam will help him overcome his enemies. Nauzar inherits the throne but quickly antagonizes his advisers and subjects. To stave off a rebellion, he summons Sam. When the old paladin arrives, Nauzar embraces him and swears to take his advice and to rule justly.

en: Folio alt38v The Court of Faridun, his mother Faranak sends gifts to her son (Cropped)

value.en: Folio alt38v The Court of Faridun, his mother Faranak sends gifts to her son (Cropped)
When the news of Faridun's enthronement spreads, his mother, Faranak, sends Arabian horses, gold, gems, and other gifts. Notables the world over offer gifts and journey to pay homage to the new king.

en: Folio 74v Zal Dictates a Letter to Sam about Rudaba

value.en: Folio 74v Zal Dictates a Letter to Sam about Rudaba

Zal calls a scribe and dictates a letter to Sam disclosing, his love for Rudaba and asking his father’s permission to wed. He reminds Sam of the oath he made upon retrieving Zal from the simurgh’s nest to grant his every wish. Dismaved with his son's choice, Sam asks his astrologers, who foretell a son of Zal and Rudaba who will save Tran from the Turanians.

en: Folio 50v Manuchihr at the Court of Faridun

value.en: Folio 50v Manuchihr at the Court of Faridun

Iraj is survived by a daughter, born after his death. When she reaches maturity, she marries a cousin of Faridun and bears a son, Manuchihr. When the child comes ofage, Faridun places him on a golden throne, assembles all the notables ofthe kingdom to pay him homage, and announces that he will lead the campaign against his grandfathers murderers.

en: Folio 342v The Fifth Joust of the Rooks Ruhham Versus Barman

value.en: Folio 342v The Fifth Joust of the Rooks Ruhham Versus Barman

The fourth joust features Faruhil, an Iranian champion known for his skill with the bow, against the Turanian Zangula. After peppering Zangula with arrows, Faruhil shoots one that hits both Zangula and his horse, which falls forward, throwing its rider. Zangula falls on his head and dies instantly.Ruhham versus Barman. Now Ruhham, son of Gudarz, is matched against the Turanian Barman. At first the champions duck and dodge one another, but soon Ruhham comes from behind Barman, stabbing him in the rear with his lance and polishing him off with a fatal puncture of the liver.

en: Folio 135v Rustam and the Seven Champions of Iran Hunt in Turan

value.en: Folio 135v Rustam and the Seven Champions of Iran Hunt in Turan

At a feast with the Seven Champions, Rustam proposes a hunt on Afrasiyab's land. After a week of the Iranians slaughtering all manner of prey, followed by feasting, and wine, Afrasiyab gets wind of the trespassers and gathers a huge army to attack them.

en: Folio 21v Hushang Slays the Black Div

value.en: Folio 21v Hushang Slays the Black Div
Hushang Slays the Black Div, from the Shah Tahmasp's Shahnama, f. 21v. Attributed to Soltan Mohammad. 321 x 215 mm. Tabriz, c. 1522-1530. The Welch Collection

en: Folio 367v The Night Battle of Kay Khusrau and Afrasiyab

value.en: Folio 367v The Night Battle of Kay Khusrau and Afrasiyab

At nightfall, after a day-long battle against Afrasiyab and his army, Kai Khusrau warned his Iranian troops that Afrasiyab would certainly try a surprise night attack. The Iranians dug a ditch across the Turanian route, doused their lights, and maintained silence. Meanwhile, Afrasiyab ordered the attack on the Iranians. When the Turanian army neared the Iranian camp, they let out war cries and charged, but many fell in the trench, and the rest fled. Then Rustam went forward from one side, Giv, Tus, and Gudarz from the other, and massacred the Turanian army. Afrasiyab abandoned his troops and fled. This was his last battle against the Iranians. Bashdan_Qara, who illustrated this scene, was one of the journeyman artists of the Shahnama, tasked most often with painting battle scenes.

en: Folio 38v The Court of Faridun, his mother Faranak sends gifts to her son

value.en: Folio 38v The Court of Faridun, his mother Faranak sends gifts to her son
When the news of Faridun's enthronement spreads, his mother, Faranak, sends Arabian horses, gold, gems, and other gifts. Notables the world over offer gifts and journey to pay homage to the new king.

en: Folio 80v Manuchihr Welcomes Sam but Orders War upon Mihrab

value.en: Folio 80v Manuchihr Welcomes Sam but Orders War upon Mihrab

Following his arrival at the imperial court, Sam recounts his military victories and is feted by Manuchihr. The next day Manuchihr refuses to speak of Rudaba and Zal and instead orders Sam to set out immediately on a mission to attack and destroy Mihrab’s palace and the surrounding city of Kabul.

en: Folio 112v Rustams First Encounter with Afrasiyab

value.en: Folio 112v Rustams First Encounter with Afrasiyab

Now that the Iranians have a new shah, they are ready to revive the battle. Rustam spies Afrasivab and charges at him, grabbing him by the belt and lifting him into the air. But victory is not to be, for the belt snaps. The Turanians rush to protect the fallen warrior and spirit him away from the battlefield. The Turanians flee and eventually sue for peace, which restores the borders of Iran to those defined by Faridun.

en: Folio 134r Kai Kavus Ascends to the Sky

value.en: Folio 134r Kai Kavus Ascends to the Sky

Kai Kavus was a long-lived powerful king and a capricious ruler. One day, while hunting, he was approached by a div disguised as a handsome youth. The ill-natured devil began praising him, saying that his royal farr–”the kingly glory–was of such splendor that he should reign the world from the heavens. Seduced by this idea, the king ordered his servants to build him a golden throne, which he then lifted up in the air with the help of four hungry eagles tied to the throne's corners at whose top were placed succulent baits of meat. The floating throne floating quickly met disaster: when the birds tired out, the flying seat–and its heedless ruler–plunged to earth. Although more sober than other illustrations, this painting displays a perfect balance of compositional rigor and humorous license. The solemn ascension of the heavily built throne is observed and commented upon by the figures in the lower register of the painting. Their amazement is expressed by the well-known gesture of bringing the finger to the mouth, as seen in the character on the left, by the kid on the far right pulling his father's tunic in an interrogatory attitude, and by the reclining pose of the figure at the center, who could appreciate the event more comfortably

en: Folio 83v Mihrab Vents His Anger Upon Sindukht

value.en: Folio 83v Mihrab Vents His Anger Upon Sindukht

When Mihrab of Kabul first heard of his daughter Rudaba’s secret love affair with Zal, he wanted to kill her, but his wife Sindukht persuaded him that Sam, Zal’s father, had agreed to the alliance. Now, when Mihrab learned that Shah Manuchihr had ordered Sam to destroy his house, Mihrab’s anger arose anew against his wife for falsely quelling his former fears. Sindukht then suggested that she visit Sam at his camp, bringing gifts from Mihrab’s treasury. Desperate to save his kingdom, Mihrab allowed his wife to undertake the mission. The decorous depiction of Sindukht and Mihrab in her chamber gives little indication of the heated emotions of their exchange.

en: Folio 243v The Battle of Pashan Begins

value.en: Folio 243v The Battle of Pashan Begins

en: Folio 25v The Death of King Mirdas

value.en: Folio 25v The Death of King Mirdas

The just king of Arabia, Mirdas(Mardas), has an ambitious son, Zahhak, who joins Iblis (Satan) in a plot to kill his father. Iblis orders a pit dug on the palace grounds, and when Mirdas seeks seclusion in the garden in the middle of the night, he falls into the pit and dies.

en: Folio 300v Bizhan Receives an Invitation through Manizhas Nurse

value.en: Folio 300v Bizhan Receives an Invitation through Manizhas Nurse

Bizhan's envious companion, Gurgin, suggests they attend a festival near Irman but over the border, in Turan, and abduct the prettiest girls. Bizhan enters a grove alone to spy on the girls, but Manizha, the daughter of Afrasiyab, notices him and sends her maid to learn all she can about him. When the maid reports that Bizhan has told her, Manizha invites him to her tent.

en: Folio 105r Afrasiyab on the Iranian Throne

value.en: Folio 105r Afrasiyab on the Iranian Throne

Back in Dahistan the Turanians defeat the Iranians and take Shah Nauzar prisoner. With news of the Turanian rout in Zabul, Afrasiyab pillories Nauzar and beheads him. The Turanians march across Iran, and Afrasiyab sits on the throne. With this victory come the vast riches of the Iranian treasury, which servants parade before him. One year later famine descends on Iran and Turan, and the two enemies are forced to agree to an armistice.

With Rustam at his side, Zal sets out to find a new shah, someone with the kingly aura denoting the true heir to the Iranian throne. On the advice of the magi, Zal and Rustam head for Mount Alburz, where Kay Qubad, a descendant of Faridun, lives. At the foot of the mountain, Rustam enters a clearing, in which he finds a magnificent youth seated on a throne. This is Kay Qubad, who has dreamed that a white falcon, Rustam will bring him forth to lead Iran.

In his final decisive battle against Salm, Manuchihr advances until he discovers Salm retreating. He slices otf Salm's head, and as his soldiers mount it on a pike, Salm's army surrenders and is granted clemency

en: Folio 87v Zal Expounds the Mysteries of the Magi

value.en: Folio 87v Zal Expounds the Mysteries of the Magi

The Shahnama made for the Safavid ruler Shah Tahmasp (r. 1524–76) is the most luxurious sixteenth‑century copy of this poem about the legendary kings of Iran. It took more than twenty years to complete and involved two generations of the best Safavid artists, including Qadimi, painter of the scene here, who worked on many projects for Shah Tahmasp. In this painting, the hero Zal seeks advice on his wish to marry Rudaba from the wise Magi, seated at the foot of his throne.
